I have 10k sequenced samples of a 3MiB memory buffer. How do determine the best small (~1k) fixed collection of addresses in the buffer to monitor if I want to spot major changes? I know that most of the 3MiB is probably functionally determined by a much smaller set.
@progrium https://t.co/weCl1D6dYk
@progrium Or you could zero the vertical component of the camera vector so that billboards don't try to follow the tilting

@santiontanon PCA would give me 1k dense vectors as the axes to project onto. I need very (0.0003%) sparse vectors. SparsePCA is my first thought but even that doesn't get the idea that I want the entire collection of addresses to sparse, not just each in isolation (as an L1 penalty would get)

@santiontanon Our previous (somewhat effective) strategy was to just keep the first 1k bytes, however this has some pathological failures. Keeping a random 1k sample would be a tiny bit better, but sometimes we can obviously see that 90% of the buffer is always blank zeros.

@santiontanon We had an entropy related idea as well but feared it would love the wildly changing bytes of framebuffer pixels rather than slowly changing but meaningful bytes such as player x/y/health (context is summarizing videogame state snapshots).

Vector analogies work for embeddings of Super Metroid gameplay screenshots! D ~= C+k(B-A)
where k=1,
A=img("Samus standing outdoors"),
B=img("Samus in morph ball outdoors"),
C=img("Samus standing indoors"),
D=img("Samus in morph ball indoors").

The effect is (just barely) not confounded by acquiring the first missile powerup in addition to the morphing ball powerup. We can't seem to construct similar ABCD for Mario (big/small Mario and early/late level) because time and score changes alter screen more than char size.
This points to a need for a special varying attention model. We stare at the character we control way more than time and score text.
Preliminary steps towards the "king - queen = man - woman" of videogames...

Technically, we found sim(D,C+k(B-A)) > sim(D,C+k(B-A)). For search-by-analogy, this means your query more closely pointed at the real target than it did at the original query. The system is looking where you point, not just looking at your finger.
Gah, I got a C and D mixed up in the tweet, but it's right in the experiment code (we think).

Last year, we introduced the problem of building search engines that understood the contents of interactive media (https://t.co/aqAnrLbR57).
This year, we're showing you how to gather up those contents automatically! https://t.co/dMZsRGimTz

Much high-profile work in automated gameplay (including reinforcement learning) aims to learn an action policy that maximizes some in-game score or win-rate. In most cases, this require enormous amounts of computational power.
https://t.co/ckKOEpHfEA

Even today, one of the biggest bottlenecks holding RL back is the problem of exploration. Learning is much faster once someone hands you a sequence of stepping stones on the path to those high scores (actions not required).
https://t.co/H9lB9zkx8X

In fact, you can get absurdly high scores if you use algorithms that simply intend to cover ground and leave policy learning for later.
https://t.co/tkHFCWxbll (we're looking forward to seeing the paper for this one)

We propose that covering interesting ground (towards no particular goal) on human-comparable timescales is the more interesting problem to solve. This can power search engines, accelerate quality-assurance testing, and enable deeper humanistic scholarship.

Playing videogames doesn't need to be a proxy or metaphor for real-world applications; it can be the main challenge if you play games for relevant platforms (Android, Unity, etc.). In our latest work, we're almost there with Nintendo 64 games.

Our focus is on extracting a ~semantic map~ of interesting states instead of an action policy. There's still learning and planning inside of this process, but these aren't the top-level goals: they serve exploration.

Here's our new paper laying out the problem, importantly including ~metrics~ for automatic exploration in unknown games and comparing these with human performance given equal in-game time: https://t.co/aX2tbWCbzI

We'll be presenting this at the Second AAAI Workshop on Knowledge Extraction from Games (https://t.co/OFnwgrFg2O) because the goal is to extract useful knowledge from a game, not to act intelligently within its walls.

If unit-radius particles accumulate onto a seed point in n-dimensional Euclidean space according to a diffusion limited aggregation process, how fast does the variance in particle positions grow as a function of time?

I'd expect it to be asymptotically faster than the variance of a single particle executing a random walk for the same time (because new positions as biased outwards), but I don't know how to set up this question formally.
Context: I'm looking for a theoretical argument that an algorithm like Rapidly-Exploring Random Trees can cover ground more efficiently than drawing random walks from a fixed seed point.

Every few weeks I try to re-clarify my ideas around a mathematical theory of play. Built out of stuff like hyperbolic embeddings of the states of zero-reward Markov decision processes, I'm trying to get it to answer why we like going down slides in playgrounds.

You are given a well-defined task, but you assume it has been slightly misspecified because of noise. Usually only a few actions are the right way to make progress towards many possible goals. Now consider the situation where the noise drowns out the signal.

Given some plausible general form for this noise, how to act when you are not explicitly rewarded for doing so becomes well-defined. I want to be able to assume the distortions are somewhat smooth on some embedding of the state-action space.

@togelius @yooceii @Amidos2006 @FilipoGiovanni If there are distractor concepts we don't want the agent to learn (e.g. which map; what absolute agent position) I'd think to use adversarial reprentation learning. Add a penalty for when the distractor concepts are predictable from the learned repr. Train like in GANs.

@togelius @yooceii @Amidos2006 @FilipoGiovanni This would robustly express the network design whereas cropping just makes it harder (but still sometimes possible) to learn to act on distractor concepts.
Mostly I'm just trying to fit this into the "no, you really don't have to engineer features anymore" DL caricature.

@togelius @FilipoGiovanni @yooceii @Amidos2006 That's like saying I'm keeping my classifier from being racist by not feeding it an explicit race feature. We know that doesn't work generally because expressive models can often learn a concept that is a proxy for the masked feature. (distinguishing learned concepts from inputs)

@FilipoGiovanni @togelius @yooceii @Amidos2006 I believe it works in specific cases (e.g. games with certain design conventions) but I'm getting at what can apply DRL applications broadly. What's the equivalent of cropping for text adventure games? For protein folding? Sticking with visuals, for RL-based image retrieval?

I can't help but interpret shape-matching work through the lens of Hofstadter https://t.co/GVrRCkkbZz If Smooth Shells is about reasoning with abstractions, Deep Shells is about learning a usefully biased vocabulary so that certain abstractions match more obviously than others. https://t.co/gYdvI34TMq

Like, we know how this fragment of the paw-out cat should match with the full shape of the crouching cat. Most algorithmic methods until recently (https://t.co/9utp9NADau) have been hypersensitive to the loc/shape of the cutting boundary, but we know to mostly ignore it. https://t.co/LTSmQXoGH6

If the cutting of the mesh is a metaphor for collecting data under selection bias, how do we get machine learning systems infer the missing global patterns and not to not cue on the peculiarities of the selection mechanism?

Image-to-image translation can be seen as shape matching where the points on 3D models are like w*h*3 tensors on the manifolds of class-specific natural images. The zebra-to-horse task is one instance. We'd like a smooth map from the zebra manifold to the horse manifold and back.

But this is like the approach taken in https://t.co/2sSHUTdJdG where (image-to-image) colorization, super-resolution, and in-painting are all learned in seconds by fitting a linear transformation to PCA-abstracted image manifolds. No complicated GAN or heap of auxiliarly losses. https://t.co/2Mum1srAnb

Just wrapped up my Machine Playtesting grad class. Here's a sample of the final research projects that were presented. Even though the class often used videogame testing as the default example, you'll see how widely the idea of machine playtesting was interpreted:

Interpreting the morality systems of several 19th century Snakes and Ladders boards using the stationary distribution of their Markov chains (players are much more likely to land on some places than others): https://t.co/1SVCzObonW
Parameter optimization for a social simulation engine towards a certain target distribution of emergent narrative events using the CMA-ES algorithm: https://t.co/8cK0GpMzjw

Investigation of different cell representations (analogous to embedding spaces used in other algs) for use in the Go-Explore automated gameplay algorithm, evaluation on multiple GameBoy games: https://t.co/oaSjEbJ788
Using VAEs to learn disentangled(-ish) moment vector representations from a corpus of game screenshots (previous methods used a pixels-to-memory pretext task): https://t.co/vuOp2D2Aum

Replaying interaction traces in cosmological dataviz tool to see how it responds to UI internationalization -- it found a real bug where translated a string was mistakenly used to build a URL, causing the app to fail to load critical data for Spanish-language users: https://t.co/GGo0kxAhQr

Each project had well-considered Intellectual Merit and Broader Impacts statements. Even if the prototype technical systems didn't always land where they were intended, they all worked in research directions that *should* be explored further.

In the Machine Playtesting grad class that just finished, in addition to insightfully discussing a variety of readings (ofc!), they completed a series of hands-on programming exercises submitted as notebooks on Google Colab:
P1: Using bulk simulation statistics to determine the effect of adding a special item to an abstracted table-top game /cc @DormansJoris https://t.co/xXsXsLU68z

P2: Using game theory / restricted play analysis infer how often each strategy in a competitive matchup game could be played while still achieving 1-ε fraction of the optimal win rate /cc @Blinkity https://t.co/EBKk1COI1h

P3: Exhaustively enumerating the statespace of a board game (modeling dice-based transition probabilities) and using the stationary distribution of the associated Markov chain to infer chances of experiencing narrative events /cc @circuitlions https://t.co/HCpis2weOm
P4: Tuning the Go-Explore automated gameplay algorithm in application to to several different GameBoy games via the OpenAI Retro API /cc @jeffclune https://t.co/m6CMA8q3Zl

P5: One last project about modeling strategic board games using MDPs for the purposes predicting impacts on optimal strategies was cut (pandemic!) in favor of giving students more time for their individually selected research projects seen in this thread: https://t.co/1jNLk5KmRV

Below are two snippets from my dissertation proposing to infer interpretable causal models of how people interact with games via abductive+inductive logic programming. Something like it existed on top of Prolog, but I wanted to build it on ASP. https://t.co/0h4rTQtfQi

Building ILP atop ASP seemed nice for supporting predicate invention and expressive biases, but the bigger idea (not then obvious) was using abduction to simultaneously *complete* the missing training data in a way that was consistent with the rules being learned.
It's probably better to propose an elaborate latent representation that evolves according to simple rules than a simple latent representation that evolves according to complex and brittle rules.

Attention in transformers: when node i has a key with value r in dimension t, it means the value for node i has likelihood ∝ exp(r) of being relevant to latent topic t. Dot(q,k) is "are you speaking on all of the topics I'm interested in?" (in a log-linear probability model).

Nodes don't really want to attend to one another -- they just want to know the general opinion on a few specific topics, giving preference to ideas on the *intersection* of topics while still considering less sophisticated takes that only overlap the union of topics.

Maybe the heads in multi-head attention are ways of issuing multiple unrelated queries in parallel. Maybe I want to read about [(cat,litter),(breakfast,recipes)] to be prepared for tomorrow, but I'm not actually seeking the intersection: [cat,litter,breakfast,recipes].
To aggregate the general opinion on some topics, maybe we should not just compute the (weighted) mean of value vectors but also the (co)variance to convey the current controversy on those topics.

Using a VQ-VAE to learn a vocabulary of 16 latent tile descriptors for an image of a Warcraft II map, generating a fresh arrangement of tile codes using WFC, and then rendering back to pixels using the learned decoder: https://t.co/UGAc88Evnu

@adrian_perez Just one, and so it only generates outputs in the style of that one. Every 2x2 chunk of tiles plagiarizes some small chunk of the input/training map, but it can use those chunks in different ways.

@wavefunk_ Pass the source image through learned encoder, now it is a grid of tile codes (codebook indexes) that work just like the raw pixels in the original WFC demos. One tile can be used next to another if it was seen to do so in the encoder source image.

@wavefunk_ As for what latent tiles represent, that's where things get interested. The learned decoder can vary the appearance of a tile based on its context (e.g. to draw n/s/e/w terrain transitions). Individual tiles don't have a fixed look. They are like phonomes rather than whole words.

@adrian_perez WFC is usually used with tiny, expertly crafted datasets (https://t.co/U6yk9ROLbz) whereas deep learning methods are usually hungry for huge messy data. This project is about showing a point between these extremes.

@togelius Nagging, or negative aggregation, is when you configure the ensemble of classifiers says no when any of the individuals in it says no (average logits rather than probs). Useful in cases of extreme class imbalance or when you really just really want to shoot down positive ideas.

@togelius Looting, or (random) linear shooting, is a reduced-order optimization technique where you replace all parameters by a (random) linear function of a smaller set. The idea is to fuck around in a subspace and find out what you can without paying the (moral) cost of dimensionality.

@togelius Ghosting (theoretically motivated by the halting problem and inspired by the recent practical successes of sleep sort) is when a model simply declines to quickly terminate with a prediction when it thinks that prediction might lead to an increase in the loss score.

@moyix Ask Prolog for lists that are equal to themselves after you append them ("append(X,X,X)"). You'll expect to see X=[], but there's more. I find the contents and order of the first few solutions to be particularly hilarious (relative to the ambient mood in logic programming).

@moyix Can't reproduce now, but the #3 result was like X=[A,B|X]: A list composed of an infinitely repeating sequences of as-yet-undefined terms A and B. Slap two of these bad boys together and indeed you get the original list again (or at least something guaranteed to unify with it).
@moyix X=[X|X] also works, but I'm not even sure how to translate this one into English.

@deepinthebuild @moyix X=[X|X] is a sequence where the head that unifies with the tail: an infinite sequence of sequences, each containing an infinite sequence of... just cons nodes all the way down. No other data. https://t.co/bLMXPlBhmE

But seriously, this paper combines my two favorite techniques for speeding up grounding:
1. Replace sparse/one-hot encodings by dense/binary encodings.
2. Targeting a beyond-NP solver and using quantification to avoid grounding-time unrolling.

Both ideas take predictable, polynomial-time grounding costs and trade them for less-predictable, worst-case-exponential-time solving costs. In theory, this is dumb. So it is super satisfying when it works in practice!

1 { pick(R,I):num(I) } 1 :- role(R).
:- #sum { B:pick(big,B); -S:pick(small,S) } < 10.

@Autumnsburg bit(0..31).
role(big;small).
{ on(R,B):bit(B) } :- role(R).
:- #sum { (2^B):on(big,B); -(2^B):on(small,B) } < 10.
#show on/2.
@Autumnsburg We've gone from O(n^2) to O(n) to O(log(n)) here. If you let me model floats, I can do O(log(log(n)).

@Autumnsburg % ufloat15
fb(e,0..4). fb(m,0..9).

var(b;s).

{ on(V,F,B):fb(F,B) } :- var(V).

s(V,B,-1) :- on(V,m,B).
s(V,B+X*(E+1),E) :- s(V,B,E-1), fb(e,E), X = { on(V,e,E) }.
s_on(V,B) :- s(V,B,3).

:- #sum { (2^B):s_on(b,B);
 -(2^B):s_on(s,B) } < 10.

#show on/3.

@Autumnsburg Constraining variance is cool, but probably overkill. You probably want visible variation rather than statistically sound sampling. Enforcing a minimum distance between samples (https://t.co/wzBkzzVRq9) might be more direct to the desired effect.

@Autumnsburg Imagine modeling coin flips. HHHHHHHH is just as likely as HTHTHTHT or any other combination likr HHHTTHHTH. If you want things to *look* random (close to equal frequencies, no runs too long or too many short runs), you have to ask for those perceptual features by name.

@Autumnsburg This is analogous to constraining length of runs for coin flips. Use one rule to detect pairwise matches, then add a constriaint to make matches don't happen too often. Constrained variance wouldn't fix ages of 7,7,7,7,45,45,45,45.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth We aren't training end to end. We train the convolutional VQ-VAE in one phase. In the next, we extract the latent codes seen by WFC using the trained encoder. This is similar to how the discrete encoder/decoder in DALL-E isn't trained at the same time as the sequence-gen xformer.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth The valid adjacency list used by WFC has the same effect. If you match the overlapping pattern size in WFC with the receptive field of the decoder/renderer, then WFC will never produce local arrangements of codes that weren't also seen in training.
@tehqin17 @isaac_karth You might think of WFC as learning a simple sequence model based on an allowlist of bi-grams, but done in the 2D case.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth I don't recognize "codebook generator" here -- are you asking about what process trains the codebook (the table of ~16 ~128-dim vectors; those points to which all outputs of the continuous encoder network are snapped/quantized)?

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth The codebook parameters are optimized via stochastic gradient descent in the same end-to-end process that sets all of the other parameters in the encoder and decoder network in that first training phase. https://t.co/rHefQ2STjC
RT @romero: Make your first level last. By then, you know the game inside and out, and you know what you need to teach the player. Above al…

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth I'm not following your ideas here. VQVAEs only have one training stage and typically don't involve transformers. The are often used to induce a discrete latent vocabulary. Once data is projected into this vocab, it can be fit by a different kind of model.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth Where others have used sequence models to capture the joint distribution of grids of codes, we use WFC in the interest of direct controllability and having very few parameters to fit.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth Metaphorically it is closer to masked language models (predict a location given all nearby context) than autoregressive models (predict next given only the context in one causal/sequence direction). You can think of WFC as using a Markov Random Field with very sloppy statistics.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth The VQ-VAE refers to just eqn 3 of https://t.co/rHefQ2STjC The separately trained prior is useful for image modeling, but it's not part of the AE. "VQ" is an aspect of the AE (eqn 3), not the prior. We also have two stages, and both papers fit the VQ-VAE in the first stage.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth The decoder in our setup isn't analogous to the generator in past work on GANs for level design. Out decoder goes from discrete code inputs to continuous pixel outputs whereas past GAN work had a generator that goes from continuous noise inputs to discrete tiles outputs.

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth The GAN generator is closer to our use of WFC: it's the part that generates fresh tile grids with plausible local patterns of tiles (eg. goomba above ground, pipe-left next to pipe-right).

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth We implement the WFC-style generator in answer-set programming (ASP). ASP lets us add constraints on the latent tile grid like prespecified tile placements, tile count bounds, or reachability along paths. These are hand-authored rather than learned from examples (a few LOC each).

@tehqin17 @isaac_karth ASP has a feature for sample diversity called "projected enumeration" where each additional solution is constrained to be different from all past solutions. In https://t.co/xMsoqaJbOD it was used to make sure each puzzle generated required a topologically distinct solution.

If you think you can ignore all this compact manifold stuff, consider that your normally distributed high-dimensional vectors are *very likely* to be concentrated on a thin hyperspherical shell. Better to imagine your data live on circles than numberlines. https://t.co/4nYDAOO5yl

You can represent a 2D vector in polar coordinates with radius number and an angle number, right? In an n-dim space, it still takes one number to represent the radius but n-1 numbers to code the angle thing. High-dim vectors are much more angle-like than radius-like.

Let's make some soy sauce! I've never done this before, and it is going to take a few months/years.

We'll start with 500g of dry soy beans (about 2½ cups). https://t.co/71UzZlEMoe
Get those beans soaking in some water. https://t.co/9Kc4dn0Y7L
If you think the cat will drink out of the bowl, cover it with something. Let it sit for a day. https://t.co/Pie6Lanx9K
A day later, the beans are oblong rather than spherical and about double  in size. Fine bubbles in the soaking water suggest some fermentation activity ahead of schedule. https://t.co/xmntSIvMBa
After a rinse with fresh water, they are into the pressure cooker for 35 minutes of "Bean" mode. https://t.co/Fu2HHvM6Fo

While I haven't planned out this whole thing yet, I'm roughly following a Korean process based on @maangchi's excellent guide to 된장 (fermented soybean paste) and 곡간장 (soup soy sauce). https://t.co/draiMnLRTt
After cooking, they should smoosh easily. Skim any floaters from the pot. https://t.co/kEgKw6uPCl
Drain the cooked beans. (The resulting broth looks useful, so I'll save a jar of it in the fridge until I can learn more about it.) https://t.co/vc1O6t0SgU
Smash! https://t.co/u9aHGXDPy3
Divide, knead to compact, then shape into blocks (메주) . Use wet hands to avoid sticky buildup as you work. https://t.co/kuGEmO5WFS

Set up a warm and breathable fermentation station, and set the blocks inside. I'm using a dish towel, a glass plate, a 30 Watt brew belt, a cardboard box inoculated with Amazon warehouse spores, some masking tape, and a bath towel. Room temperature is 75°F/24°C right now. https://t.co/wcNZB4Sjd9

What does it taste like at this point? It's very bland but still distinctly soy-y, like tofu. It clearly has the texture of something that will eventually become doenjang, but it is a bit fibrous and cardboardy on the tongue. Maybe the bean hulls will break down in fermentation.

Thermal upgrades: I was getting too much heat by direct contact with the bottom of the box. The meju are now airgapped with chopstick spacers. I installed a temperature monitor, and I put the heater on an switched outlet with a 50% duty cycle timer schedule. https://t.co/1Iq2L51lA6

The timer unfortunately turns off the temperature monitor as well, but I can still get a general idea. The air temperature in the box swings from 35°C to 45°C. This is hot compared to traditional methods, but not unreasonable according to the biochemistry literature.
Drying, cracking, and showing some early signs of the desired microbial life. I'll probably keep it warmed and covered in the box for another week. https://t.co/VGayGe3srp

I've always wondered how different masting years were from non-masting years. The distribution seems smoother than I expected after I sorted the bars in the chart from this article. (analysis: https://t.co/KNF29yIILC) https://t.co/jWUinUkK5X https://t.co/Q1DWyUYA07

Olfactory update: The blocks have gone from a barely detectable earthy smell into the wet sock direction with a sharp attack of ammonia (familiar from Brie cheese). The smell is faint at the source, but it can build up in the box over the course of a day.

Yup, those are some moldy blocks. It's going to be pretty warm outside for the next few days, so I'll go onto the next stage. (This avoids keeping that electric heater running in my already too-warm kitchen.) https://t.co/lV9DXv25vq
Now they just hang out on the eaves of the front porch to ward off visitors during the pandemic. https://t.co/eeIo92cCxl
It's a good idea to label and date your fermentation projects. https://t.co/pNPZOZ78gX

In the early 2000s, the instructor for my AI class told us that AI is mostly about 5 things (with an oddly memorable acronym pronounced "rickles"): Retrieval, Inference, Learning, Knowledge representation, and Search. They added that Robotics should be included in there too.

On the LLM discourse: There's some interesting Learning happening during the training phase (particularly how Inference is leveraged during Learning), and for the fixed systems that emerge from the training phase there is some interesting Learning-as-Inference happening.

But if we want to understand these curious mechanisms so we can reliably build with them (in ways that are orthogonal to advancing centralized data collection), I don't think we should be trying to build bigger and bigger systems.

If we want to understand how fitting Big Curves during training relates to useful in-context-learning and surprising novelty by interpolation during the inference phase, lets dig into kriging (https://t.co/Pwoq5YKzxh).

While we're at it, can we get a handle on these web-scale datasets from a Knowledge Representation and Retrieval perspective? For-loops over unsorted string archives (to feed mini-batch gradient descent) surely isn't the best we can do.

Synthesizing a boolean function with two 8-bit inputs and a 1-bit output:
1. training spec
2. enforce spatial consistency (100% accuracy but chaotic)
3. uncertainty from bootstrap consensus
4. bagging (can disagree with training data; bias–variance tradeoff)
Result: 197-node BDD. https://t.co/gTUKWAViBz

This stuff feels kinda like training random forests, but there's no randomness and no trees. No loss, no optimization, no epochs nor early stopping (single pass). No comparisons, no multiplications. Just bits.

@boris_brave Related to locality sensitive hashing, partition the space by the least significant bits of the input variables. Split by four: [x0&y0, x0&~y0, ~x0&y0, ~x0&~y0]. Repeat this trick to get sparser and sparser constraints that can be modeled by simpler and simpler circuits.

Suppose you have n bits of information that you need to summarize for later inference with only O(log(n)) bits of memory. You could store any combination of...
- a constant number of pointers into the sequence
- a logarithmic number of global flags
- ...

- a Piecewise Constant Function with a constant number of switchpoints
- a PCF with a logarithmic number of switchpoints identified by fixed precision
- a precise count matches for a fixed pattern vocabulary
- a fixed-precision count of a logarithmic number of patterns
- ...

- probably some richer functions under smoothness constraints
- ... what else?

- a graph with a logarithmic number of nodes (and anything you can build from graphs with constant-sized labels)
- a logarithmic-sized expression tree over a constant symbol vocabulary
- those graphs augmented with a constant number of special nodes having log-sized labels
...

Generate-and-test pipelines discard many candidate designs that don't pass a filtering test. So why don't we just run the tester first and generate within the space that remains? (This isn't a joke, but I'm having a hard time finding the right words for it.)

I often use symbolic methods for machine playtesting. Given a design, I'll make sure there's a good path through it and that there are no bad paths. With these methods, the design doesn't actually need to be known up front. You can finish the analysis before generation starts.

The result of the analysis is this giant symbolic expression involving all of your design variables. From there, you plug in the analysis result you want and then solve for values of the design variables that would give you that result.

If you were using BDDs for all of this, solving for the design given the result is a (fixed-parameter) constant time operation. For this little grid-world platformer scenario I'm testing (a toy, but not trivial), the guaranteed-test-passing results come out in *microseconds*.

@LukeD I think you are saying that we can design the generator to make the tester's job is easier. This is the common workflow, but I'm proposing the reverse. I want to design the tester to make the generator's job is easier. This is going to work because I have this weird new testing.

@MatthewGuz I hadn't, but it was a refreshing read this morning. I like the idea of deriving the shape of the space from the evaluation function, and I'm doing something like the glass-box version of that and without iterative improvement (because I have a filter rather than score fn).

@mtrc That's it. We'll have the same generative space as exhaustive PCG, but we'll store a compressed representation of the space of results rather than enumerate them explicitly. Unlike solver-based methods, however, we'll do most of the compute up front so that sampling is cheap.

@mtrc "pre-compiled design space models" is the phrase floating around my head recently. You write declarative rules like in a solver-based setup, you pre-compute like in a QD archive, and you sample by walking a tree-like data structure as if it were an unconstrained design grammar.

I'm excited about this allowing us to switch to byte-level tokens without a major slowdown. That would better set us up for constraining the output by a grammar (e.g. file format). https://t.co/Pu4gWPGj8p

Heuristic: Your train–test investment ratio should be like the reciprocal of your train–test application ratio. If the model will run a billion times after training just once, you might accept having training cost a billion times more than one prediction. https://t.co/8GIQ2mPpaF

So, if you are trying to build a chatgpt-like product accessible to a global everyday audience, it'd be irresponsible *not* to have a cost ratio like 100 billion. If your goal is to push the boundaries of AI capability, you *ought* to kick the balance back several orders of mag.
